name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلْمُقَرَّبُونَ (١١)
```

```english
they are the ones brought near [to Allah], (11)
```

```arabic
فِى جَنَّـٰتِ ٱلنَّعِيمِ (١٢)
```

```english
[who will reside] in the gardens of bliss. (12)
```

```arabic
ثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (١٣)
```

```english
A multitude from the former [generations] (13)
```

```arabic
وَقَلِيلٌ مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (١٤)
```

```english
and a few from the latter ones. (14)
```

```arabic
عَلَىٰ سُرُرٍ مَّوْضُونَةٍ (١٥)
```

```english
On brocaded couches (15)
```

```arabic
مُّتَّكِـِٔينَ عَلَيْهَا مُتَقَـٰبِلِينَ (١٦)
```

```english
reclining on them, face to face. (16)
```

```arabic
يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدَٰنٌ مُّخَلَّدُونَ (١٧)
```

```english
They will be waited upon by immortal youths, (17)
```

```arabic
بِأَكْوَابٍ وَأَبَارِيقَ وَكَأْسٍ مِّن مَّعِينٍ (١٨)
```

```english
with goblets and ewers and a cup of a clear wine, (18)
```

```arabic
لَّا يُصَدَّعُونَ عَنْهَا وَلَا يُنزِفُونَ (١٩)
```

```english
which causes them neither headache nor stupefaction, (19)
```

```arabic
وَفَـٰكِهَةٍ مِّمَّا يَتَخَيَّرُونَ (٢٠)
```

```english
and such fruits as they prefer (20)
```

```arabic
وَلَحْمِ طَيْرٍ مِّمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ (٢١)
```

```english
and such flesh of fowls as they desire, (21)
```

```arabic
وَحُورٌ عِينٌ (٢٢)
```

```english
and big-eyed houris (22)
```

```arabic
كَأَمْثَـٰلِ ٱللُّؤْلُؤِ ٱلْمَكْنُونِ (٢٣)
```

```english
like guarded pearls, (23)
```

```arabic
جَزَآءًۢ بِمَا كَانُوا۟ يَعْمَلُونَ (٢٤)
```

```english
a reward for what they used to do. (24)
```

```arabic
لَا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيهَا لَغْوًا وَلَا تَأْثِيمًا (٢٥)
```

```english
They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech, (25)
```

```arabic
إِلَّا قِيلًا سَلَـٰمًا سَلَـٰمًا (٢٦)
```

```english
but only the watchword, ‘Peace!’ ‘Peace!’ (26)
```

```arabic
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْيَمِينِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٢٧)
```

```english
And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand?! (27)
```

```arabic
فِى سِدْرٍ مَّخْضُودٍ (٢٨)
```

```english
Amid thornless lote trees (28)
```

```arabic
وَطَلْحٍ مَّنضُودٍ (٢٩)
```

```english
and clustered spathes (29)
```

```arabic
وَظِلٍّ مَّمْدُودٍ (٣٠)
```

```english
and extended shade, (30)
```

```arabic
وَمَآءٍ مَّسْكُوبٍ (٣١)
```

```english
and ever-flowing water (31)
```

```arabic
وَفَـٰكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ (٣٢)
```

```english
and abundant fruits, (32)
```

```arabic
لَّا مَقْطُوعَةٍ وَلَا مَمْنُوعَةٍ (٣٣)
```

```english
neither inaccessible, nor forbidden, (33)
```

```arabic
وَفُرُشٍ مَّرْفُوعَةٍ (٣٤)
```

```english
and noble spouses. (34)
```

```arabic
إِنَّآ أَنشَأْنَـٰهُنَّ إِنشَآءً (٣٥)
```

```english
We have created them with a special creation, (35)
```

```arabic
فَجَعَلْنَـٰهُنَّ أَبْكَارًا (٣٦)
```

```english
and made them virgins, (36)
```

```arabic
عُرُبًا أَتْرَابًا (٣٧)
```

```english
loving, of a like age, (37)
```

```arabic
لِّأَصْحَـٰبِ ٱلْيَمِينِ (٣٨)
```

```english
for the People of the Right Hand. (38)
```

```arabic
ثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٣٩)
```

```english
A multitude from the former [generations] (39)
```

```arabic
وَثُلَّةٌ مِّنَ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٤٠)
```

```english
and a multitude from the latter [ones]. (40)
```

```arabic
وَأَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلشِّمَالِ مَآ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلشِّمَالِ (٤١)
```

```english
And the People of the Left Hand—what are the People of the Left Hand?! (41)
```

```arabic
فِى سَمُومٍ وَحَمِيمٍ (٤٢)
```

```english
Amid infernal miasma and boiling water (42)
```

```arabic
وَظِلٍّ مِّن يَحْمُومٍ (٤٣)
```

```english
and the shadow of a dense black smoke, (43)
```

```arabic
لَّا بَارِدٍ وَلَا كَرِيمٍ (٤٤)
```

```english
neither cool nor beneficial. (44)
```

```arabic
إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ قَبْلَ ذَٰلِكَ مُتْرَفِينَ (٤٥)
```

```english
Indeed they had been affluent before this, (45)
```

```arabic
وَكَانُوا۟ يُصِرُّونَ عَلَى ٱلْحِنثِ ٱلْعَظِيمِ (٤٦)
```

```english
and they used to persist in the great sin. (46)
```

```arabic
وَكَانُوا۟ يَقُولُونَ أَئِذَا مِتْنَا وَكُنَّا تُرَابًا وَعِظَـٰمًا أَءِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ (٤٧)
```

```english
And they used to say, ‘What! When we are dead and become dust and bones, shall we be resurrected?! (47)
```

```arabic
أَوَءَابَآؤُنَا ٱلْأَوَّلُونَ (٤٨)
```

```english
And our forefathers too?!’ (48)
```

```arabic
قُلْ إِنَّ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ وَٱلْـَٔاخِرِينَ (٤٩)
```

```english
Say, ‘Indeed the former and latter generations (49)
```

```arabic
لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلَىٰ مِيقَـٰتِ يَوْمٍ مَّعْلُومٍ (٥٠)
```

```english
will all be gathered for the tryst of a known day. (50)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا ٱلضَّآلُّونَ ٱلْمُكَذِّبُونَ (٥١)
```

```english
Then indeed, you, astray deniers, (51)
```

```arabic
لَـَٔاكِلُونَ مِن شَجَرٍ مِّن زَقُّومٍ (٥٢)
```

```english
will surely eat from the Zaqqum tree (52)
```

```arabic
فَمَالِـُٔونَ مِنْهَا ٱلْبُطُونَ (٥٣)
```

```english
and stuff your bellies with it, (53)
```

```arabic
فَشَـٰرِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ ٱلْحَمِيمِ (٥٤)
```

```english
and drink boiling water on top of it, (54)
```

```arabic
فَشَـٰرِبُونَ شُرْبَ ٱلْهِيمِ (٥٥)
```

```english
drinking like thirsty camels.’ (55)
```

```arabic
هَـٰذَا نُزُلُهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ (٥٦)
```

```english
Such will be the hospitality they receive on the Day of Retribution. (56)
```

## (Commentary)

The verses detail the final destination of each of the three groups (الأزواج الثلاثة) on the Day of Resurrection.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ» — *“They are the ones brought near [to Allah], in the Gardens of Bliss” (56:11-12).* The demonstrative pronoun "those" (أُولئِكَ) refers to "the foremost" (السابقون). The phrase "Those are the ones brought near" (أُولئِكَ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ) consists of a subject and a predicate, and the sentence is an introductory one (استئنافية). It has also been said that it is the predicate for His saying: "And the foremost" (وَالسَّابِقُونَ), and it has been said that it is a subject whose predicate is "in the Gardens of Bliss" (فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ). The first of these three views is the most appropriate considering the context of dividing people into three groups first, and then detailing the final outcome of each.

Nearness (القرب) and farness (البعد) are two relative concepts (معنيان متضائفان) that characterize bodies according to spatial relation (النسبة المكانية). Later, their usage was expanded to include non-spatial matters such as time and the like; for instance, it is said: "Tomorrow is near to today," "Four is nearer to three than five," and "Green is nearer to black than white." Subsequently, their usage was further expanded to include realities beyond bodies and corporeal things (الجسمانيات).

Nearness has been considered an attribute of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of His encompassing (الإحاطة) of all things. He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِذا سَأَلَكَ عِبادِي عَنِّي فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ** — *“When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost” (2:186)*; and He said: **وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْكُمْ** — *“and We are nearer to him than you” (56:85)*; and He said: **وَنَحْنُ أَقْرَبُ إِلَيْهِ مِنْ حَبْلِ الْوَرِيدِ** — *“and We are nearer to him than his jugular vein” (50:16).* This meaning—namely, that He, the Exalted, is nearer to a thing than the thing is to itself—is the most wondrous conception of nearness imaginable, and we have alluded to its conceptualization in the commentary on that verse.

Nearness is also considered an attribute of the servants in the stage of servitude (العبودية). Since it is an acquired matter (اكتسابيا), the term "seeking nearness" (التقرب) is used; thus, the servant seeks nearness to Allah, the Glorified, through righteous deeds (صالح العمل), which means placing oneself in the path of divine mercy by removing the causes of wretchedness and deprivation. Allah, the Glorified, brings the servant near in the sense of granting him a station where he is uniquely entitled to receive what others do not—namely, His honor, forgiveness, and mercy. He, the Exalted, said: **كِتابٌ مَرْقُومٌ يَشْهَدُهُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** — *“A written book, witnessed by those brought near” (83:20-21)*; and He said: **وَمِزاجُهُ مِنْ تَسْنِيمٍ عَيْناً يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** — *“and its mixture is of Tasnim, a spring where those brought near drink” (83:27-28).*

Thus, those brought near (المقربون) are the highest echelon of the people of felicity, as indicated by His saying: "And the foremost, the foremost; those are the ones brought near." This cannot be achieved except through the perfection of servitude (العبودية), as He said: **لَنْ يَسْتَنْكِفَ الْمَسِيحُ أَنْ يَكُونَ عَبْداً لِلَّهِ وَلَا الْمَلائِكَةُ الْمُقَرَّبُونَ** — *“The Messiah would never disdain being a servant of Allah, nor would the angels brought near” (4:172).* Servitude is not perfected unless the servant is purely subordinate in his will and action to his Master, neither willing nor acting except as He wills. This is the entry into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah; these are the friends (أولياء) of Allah.

His saying: "In the Gardens of Bliss" (فِي جَنَّاتِ النَّعِيمِ) means that every one of them is in a Garden of Bliss, so collectively they are in the Gardens of Bliss. It is also possible that it means each of them is in multiple Gardens of Bliss, but this is made unlikely by His saying at the end of the Surah: **فَأَمَّا إِنْ كانَ مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ فَرَوْحٌ وَرَيْحانٌ وَجَنَّةُ نَعِيمٍ** — *“Then, if he be of those brought near, there shall be ease and fragrance and a garden of bliss” (56:88-89).*

It has been mentioned more than once that bliss (النعيم) is the guardianship (الولاية) — *the divine authority and intimacy between Allah and His servant* — and that the Garden of Bliss is the Garden of Guardianship, which is consistent with what was just mentioned: that those brought near are the people of Allah's guardianship.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَقَلِيلٌ مِنَ الْآخِرِينَ» — *“A large group from the former peoples, and a few from the later ones” (56:13-14).* The large group (الثلة) is, as has been said, a numerous assembly. What is meant by "the former peoples" (الأولين) are the past nations of the previous prophets, and by "the later ones" (الآخرين) is this Ummah, as is customary in His speech whenever He mentions the former and the later together. Among such instances is what will follow in His saying: **أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ أَوَآباؤُنَا الْأَوَّلُونَ قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلى مِيقاتِ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ** — *“‘Shall we really be resurrected? And our forefathers of old?’ Say, ‘Indeed the former and the latter [generations] will all be gathered for the tryst of a known day’” (56:47-50).* Thus, the meaning of the two verses is: they—meaning those brought near—are a numerous group from the past nations and a few from this Ummah.

From what has preceded, it becomes clear that the view of some—that the "former" and "later" refer to the beginning and the end of this Ummah—is incorrect.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «عَلى سُرُرٍ مَوْضُونَةٍ مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَيْها مُتَقابِلِينَ» — *“On gold-webbed couches, reclining on them, facing one another” (56:15-16).* The term "webbed" (الوضن) refers to weaving, and it is said to mean the weaving of a coat of mail; its application to the weaving of couches is a metaphor (استعارة) intended to describe the firmness and intricacy of their weave.

His saying: "Reclining on them" (مُتَّكِئِينَ عَلَيْها) is a circumstantial qualifier (حال) for the pronoun referring to those brought near, and the pronoun "them" refers to the couches. His saying: "Facing one another" (مُتَقابِلِينَ) is another circumstantial qualifier for them, or for the pronoun in "reclining." Their facing one another is a metaphor for the height of their intimacy, the excellence of their social interaction, and the purity of their inner selves; they do not look at the back of their companion, nor do they find fault with him or backbite him.

The meaning is: they—the ones brought near—are settled upon woven couches while reclining on them and facing one another.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «يَطُوفُ عَلَيْهِمْ وِلْدانٌ مُخَلَّدُونَ» — *“They will be waited upon by immortal youths” (56:17).* Youths (الولدان) is the plural of child (ولد), meaning a young boy. Their "circling" (طواف) around them is a metaphor for serving them. The "immortal" (المخلدون) is from immortality (الخلود) meaning permanence; i.e., they remain forever in their state of youthful freshness. It has also been said it is from *al-khuld* (الخلد) with two *fathahs*, meaning an earring, implying they are adorned with earrings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «بِأَكْوابٍ وَأَبارِيقَ وَكَأْسٍ مِنْ مَعِينٍ» — *“with goblets, and ewers, and a cup from a flowing spring” (56:18).* Goblets (الأكواب) is the plural of *kub*, which is a vessel without a handle or a spout. Ewers (الأباريق) is the plural of *ibriq*, which is a vessel that has a spout, and it is said to have both a handle and a spout. The cup (الكأس) is well-known; it is said the cup is mentioned in the singular because it is not called a *ka's* unless it is full. What is meant by "flowing spring" (المعين) is flowing wine that is clear to the sight and running.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «لا يُصَدَّعُونَ عَنْها وَلا يُنْزِفُونَ» — *“they will not cause them any headache, nor will they be intoxicated” (56:19).* That is, they are not seized by a headache due to a hangover resulting from the wine, as happens with the wine of the world, nor does their intellect vanish through the intoxication resulting from it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَفاكِهَةٍ مِمَّا يَتَخَيَّرُونَ وَلَحْمِ طَيْرٍ مِمَّا يَشْتَهُونَ» — *“and such fruits as they may choose, and such flesh of fowls as they may desire” (56:20-21).* The fruits and the fowl are coordinated (معطوفان) with His saying: "with goblets." The meaning is: the youths circle around them with fruits of their choosing and with the flesh of birds they desire.

One should not find a problem with what has been mentioned in the narrations—that when the people of Paradise desire a fruit, the branch of its tree bends down to them with its fruit so they may take it, and when they desire the flesh of a bird, it falls into their hands fried or roasted, they eat what they wish from it, and then it returns to life and flies away.

This is because they have whatever they wish, and among the varieties of enjoyment (التنعم) is receiving what they desire from the hands of their servants, especially during their gatherings and celebrations, just as another variety is taking it themselves without the mediation of servants.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَحُورٌ عِينٌ كَأَمْثالِ اللُّؤْلُؤِ الْمَكْنُونِ» — *“and [there will be] wide-eyed houris, like preserved pearls” (56:22-23).* This is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate is omitted as indicated by the context; the estimation (التقدير) is: "and for them are wide-eyed houris" or "and therein are wide-eyed houris." The wide-eyed houris (الحور العين) are the women of Paradise, and the meaning of this term has already been explained in the commentary on Surah al-Dukhan.

His saying: "Like preserved pearls" (كَأَمْثالِ اللُّؤْلُؤِ الْمَكْنُونِ) means pearls that are protected and stored in shells, untouched by hands, thus reaching the ultimate degree of purity.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «جَزاءً بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ» — *“as a reward for what they used to do” (56:24).* This is a restriction for all that preceded and functions as an object of purpose (مفعول له). The meaning is: We have done for them what We did so that it may be a reward for them in exchange for the righteous deeds they used to perform consistently.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «لا يَسْمَعُونَ فِيها لَغْواً وَلا تَأْثِيماً» — *“They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech” (56:25).* Vain talk (اللغو) is speech that has no benefit or resulting effect. Sinful speech (التأثيم) is the attribution of sin; i.e., none of them addresses his companion with something useless, nor does he attribute sin to him, for there is no sin there. Some have interpreted *ta'thim* as lying.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِلَّا قِيلاً سَلاماً سَلاماً» — *“but only the words, ‘Peace! Peace!’” (56:26).* This is a disconnected exception (استثناء منقطع) from vain talk and sinful speech. The word *qilan* (قيلاً) is a verbal noun like *qawl* (speech). "Peace" (سَلاماً) is an explanation for His saying "words" (قيلاً), and its repetition indicates the recurrence of its occurrence. The meaning is: except for speech that is "Peace" after "Peace."

It has been said that "Peace" (سَلاماً) could be a verbal noun acting as an adjective for "words" (قيلاً), meaning: except for speech that is peaceful (سالم).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَأَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ ما أَصْحابُ الْيَمِينِ» — *“And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand?” (56:27).* This begins the detail of the final state of the "People of the Right Hand" (أصحاب الميمنة). By substituting it with "People of the Right" (أصحاب اليمين), it is known that the "People of the Right Hand" and the "People of the Right" are one and the same—they are those who are given their book in their right hand. The sentence is interrogative, intended to magnify their status and express wonder at their condition; it is the predicate for His saying: "And the People of the Right."

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فِي سِدْرٍ مَخْضُودٍ» — *“Amidst thornless lote trees” (56:28).* The lote tree (السدر) is the *nabq* tree. "Thornless" (المخضود) is that whose thorns have been cut off so it has no thorns.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَطَلْحٍ مَنْضُودٍ» — *“and clustered acacias” (56:29).* The acacia (الطلخ) is the banana tree; it is also said it is not the banana but a tree with a cool, moist shade. Others say it is the *Umm Ghaylan* tree which has fragrant blossoms. "Clustered" (نضد) means things placed one upon another. The meaning is: amidst banana trees with fruit clustered one upon another from bottom to top.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَظِلٍّ مَمْدُودٍ وَماءٍ مَسْكُوبٍ» — *“and extended shade, and ever-flowing water” (56:30-31).* It is said that the "extended" (الممدود) shade is the permanent one that is not abrogated by the sun, so it remains and does not vanish. The "ever-flowing" (المسكوب) water is that which is poured and running without interruption.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَفاكِهَةٍ كَثِيرَةٍ لا مَقْطُوعَةٍ وَلا مَمْنُوعَةٍ» — *“and abundant fruits, neither intercepted, nor forbidden” (56:32-33).* That is, not cut off (لا مَقْطُوعَةٍ) in certain seasons like the cessation of fruits in winter and the like in the world; nor forbidden (لا مَمْنُوعَةٍ) from being taken due to an internal obstacle like boredom or satiety, or an external one like distance of location, thorns preventing picking, or anything else.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَفُرُشٍ مَرْفُوعَةٍ» — *“and uplifted couches” (56:34).* Couches (الفرش) is the plural of *firash*, which is a carpet or bedding. "Uplifted" (المرفوعة) means high. It has also been said that "uplifted couches" refers to women who are elevated in status regarding their intellect, beauty, and perfection, as a woman is sometimes called a *firash*. This meaning is consistent with His saying afterward: "Indeed, We have created them with a [special] creation..." etc.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِنَّا أَنْشَأْناهُنَّ إِنْشاءً فَجَعَلْناهُنَّ أَبْكاراً عُرُباً أَتْراباً» — *“Indeed We have created them with a [special] creation, and made them virgins, loving, of equal age” (56:35-37).* That is, We have brought them into existence, originated them, and nurtured them with a special origination and nurturing. This alludes to the fact that their state does not change with youth or old age, nor in the radiance of their appearance or otherwise. His saying: "and made them virgins" (فَجَعَلْناهُنَّ أَبْكاراً) means We created them as virgins; whenever their husbands come to them, they find them virgins.

His saying: "loving, of equal age" (عُرُباً أَتْراباً). *'Urub* (العرب) is the plural of *'arub*, which is the woman who is affectionate toward her husband, or coquettish, or deeply in love with her husband. *Atrab* (الأتراب) is the plural of *tirb* (with *kasrah* then *sukun*), meaning a peer; i.e., they are peers to one another or peers in age to their husbands.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «لِأَصْحابِ الْيَمِينِ ثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَثُلَّةٌ مِنَ الْآخِرِينَ» — *“for the People of the Right Hand: a large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones” (56:38-40).* Its meaning becomes clear from what has preceded. It is inferred from the verses that the People of the Right Hand among the later ones are a numerous group just like the former ones, but the foremost ones brought near (السابقين المقربين) among the later ones are fewer in number than those among the former ones.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَأَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ ما أَصْحابُ الشِّمالِ» — *“And the People of the Left Hand—what are the People of the Left Hand?” (56:41).* A subject and a predicate; the interrogation is for expressing wonder and terror. The "People of the Ill-omened" (أصحاب المشأمة) has been substituted with "People of the Left Hand" (أصحاب الشمال) to indicate that they are those who are given their book in their left hand, as seen in the parallel case of the People of the Right.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فِي سَمُومٍ وَحَمِيمٍ وَظِلٍّ مِنْ يَحْمُومٍ لا بارِدٍ وَلا كَرِيمٍ» — *“amidst scorching winds and scalding water, and the shadow of a pitch-black smoke, neither cool nor pleasant” (56:42-44).* Scorching wind (السموم)—according to *al-Kashshaf*—is the heat of fire that penetrates the pores. Scalding water (الحميم) is water of intense heat. The nunation (تنوين) in both is for magnifying the matter. Pitch-black smoke (اليحموم) is black smoke. His saying: "neither cool nor pleasant" (لا بارِدٍ وَلا كَرِيمٍ) apparently describes the "shadow" (الظل) and not the "pitch-black smoke," because a shadow is what is expected to provide cooling by seeking shade and rest within it, unlike smoke.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا قَبْلَ ذلِكَ مُتْرَفِينَ» — *“Indeed they were affluent before this” (56:45).* This is a justification for the settlement of the People of the Left in punishment. The "this" refers to the mentioned punishment on the Day of Resurrection. Affluence (إتراف) of a blessing makes a person ungrateful and tyrannical; it occupies his soul such that he becomes heedless of what lies beyond it. Thus, a person being "affluent" (مترف) means his attachment to the worldly blessings he possesses and what he seeks from them, whether they be many or few.

Therefore, the objection that many of the People of the Left were not "affluent" in the sense of having expansive luxury is not valid. This is because man is surrounded by the blessings of his Lord, and a blessing is not merely wealth. Thus, his preoccupation with the blessings of his Lord away from his Lord is a form of "affluence" (ترفه) on his part. The meaning is: We only punish them with what was mentioned because they were previously in the world ungrateful and tyrannical due to the blessings.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَكانُوا يُصِرُّونَ عَلَى الْحِنْثِ الْعَظِيمِ» — *“and they used to persist in the great sin” (56:46).* In *al-Majma'*: *al-Hinth* (الحنث) is the breaking of a covenant confirmed by an oath, and persistence (الإصرار) is to remain upon it without desisting. End quote. Perhaps what is understood from the context is that their persistence in the "great sin" is their arrogance against the servitude (عبودية) to their Lord, which they had covenanted with Allah upon according to their primordial nature (الفطرة) and for which the pledge was taken from them in the world of particles (عالم الذر); thus they obey other than their Lord, which is absolute polytheism (الشرك المطلق).

It has been said that *al-hinth* is the great sin, so its description as "great" is for emphasis, and the "great sin" is polytheism (الشرك) with Allah. It has also been said that the "great sin" refers to the category of major sins. Others say it is the oath to deny the Resurrection, alluded to in His saying: **وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لا يَبْعَثُ اللهُ مَنْ يَمُوتُ** — *“They swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths: ‘Allah will not resurrect those who die!’” (16:38).* However, the wording of the verse is general.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَكانُوا يَقُولُونَ أَإِذا مِتْنا وَكُنَّا تُراباً وَعِظاماً أَإِنَّا لَمَبْعُوثُونَ أَوَآباؤُنَا الْأَوَّلُونَ» — *“and they used to say, ‘What! When we are dead and have become dust and bones, shall we really be resurrected? And our forefathers of old?’” (56:47-48).* This is a statement of theirs based on deeming it improbable (الاستبعاد); hence they emphasized the improbability of their own resurrection with the resurrection of their forefathers, because the improbability in their case is even more emphatic. The estimation is: "Shall our forefathers of old also be resurrected?"

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قُلْ إِنَّ الْأَوَّلِينَ وَالْآخِرِينَ لَمَجْمُوعُونَ إِلى مِيقاتِ يَوْمٍ مَعْلُومٍ» — *“Say, ‘Indeed the former and the latter [generations] will all be gathered for the tryst of a known day’” (56:49-50).* This is a command from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to respond to their deeming the Resurrection improbable by affirming it, and then informing them of what they will live on during the Day of Resurrection in terms of food and drink—namely, *Zaqqum* and *Hamim*.

The gist of the statement is that the former and the latter—without distinction between them, unlike how they distinguished by making their own resurrection improbable and the resurrection of their forefathers even more improbable and emphatic—will all be gathered and assembled for the tryst of a known day.

The tryst (الميقات) is that by which a thing is timed, which is its appointed time. What is meant by a "known day" is the Day of Resurrection, which is known to Allah; thus, the addition of "tryst" to "known day" is explanatory (بيانية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ثُمَّ إِنَّكُمْ أَيُّهَا الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ لَآكِلُونَ مِنْ شَجَرٍ مِنْ زَقُّومٍ فَمالِؤُنَ مِنْهَا الْبُطُونَ» — *“Then indeed you, O astray deniers, will surely eat from the Zaqqum tree, and fill your bellies with it” (56:51-53).* This is part of the completion of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) speech, informing them of what their state will end in on the Day of Resurrection and what they will live on in terms of food and drink.

In addressing them as "astray deniers" (الضَّالُّونَ الْمُكَذِّبُونَ), there is an allusion to the basis of their wretchedness and loss on the Day of Resurrection, which is their straying from the path of truth and the settling of that in their souls through their persistence in denial and their insistence on the "great sin." Had they been merely astray without denial, there would have been hope for them to be saved and not perish.

The "from" (مِنْ) in His saying "from a tree" (مِنْ شَجَرٍ) is for beginning (للابتداء), and in His saying "of Zaqqum" (مِنْ زَقُّومٍ) it is explanatory (بيانية); it is also possible that "of Zaqqum" is a substitute (بدل) for "from a tree." The pronoun "it" (مِنْهَا) refers to the tree or the fruit, and both can be treated as feminine or masculine; hence the feminine pronoun is used here, while in the following verse in His saying "drinking upon it" (فَشارِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ), the masculine pronoun is used. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَشارِبُونَ عَلَيْهِ مِنَ الْحَمِيمِ فَشارِبُونَ شُرْبَ الْهِيمِ» — *“and drink scalding water on top of it, drinking like thirsty camels” (56:54-55).* The word "upon" (على) denotes superiority (للاستعلاء) and in this context implies that the drinking follows the eating without delay. *Al-Him* (الهيم) is the plural of *hayma'*, referring to camels afflicted with *huyam* (with a *dammah* on the *ha*), which is a disease similar to dropsy that affects camels, causing them to drink water until they die or become severely ill. It is also said that *al-him* refers to sands that are never satiated with water.

The meaning is: then drinking, following what you have eaten of the *Zaqqum*, of the intensely hot water, drinking like the drinking of thirsty camels or like the drinking of thirsty sands. This is the end of what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was commanded to say to them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «هذا نُزُلُهُمْ يَوْمَ الدِّينِ» — *“This will be their hospitality on the Day of Retribution” (56:56).* That is, the Day of Recompense. Hospitality (النزل) is what is presented to a descending guest in terms of food and drink as an honor to him. The meaning is: this which has been mentioned of their food and drink is the "hospitality" of the astray deniers; thus, naming what has been prepared for them as "hospitality" is a form of sarcasm (تهكم). The verse is from the speech of Him, the Exalted, addressing the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); had it been from the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) addressing them, it would have said: "This is your hospitality."

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Mardawayh and Ibn 'Asakir narrated via 'Urwah ibn Ruwaym from Jabir ibn 'Abdullah who said: "When *'When the Imminent Event befalls'* was revealed, and therein was mentioned: *'A large group from the former peoples, and a few from the later ones'*, 'Umar said: 'O Messenger of Allah, a large group from the former and a few from the later?' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Come and listen to what Allah has revealed—*“A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones”*.'

Behold, from Adam to me is a large group, and my Ummah is a large group—and we shall not complete our group until we seek help from the blacks, the tenders of camels—from those who bear witness that there is no god but Allah, alone without partner." Al-Suyuti said: "Ibn Abi Hatim narrated it from another way from 'Urwah ibn Ruwaym in a disconnected (مرسلا) form." And therein: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Abu Hurayrah who said: "When *'A large group from the former peoples, and a few from the later ones'* was revealed, the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) were saddened and said: 'Then there will be only a few from the Ummah of Muhammad.' So at midday there was revealed: *'A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones'*; you will equal the people, so the verse *'and a few from the later ones'* was abrogated."

**I say:** In *al-Kashshaf*, regarding the commentary on the verse, it says: "If you say: It has been narrated that when it was revealed, it was hard upon the Muslims, so the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) kept appealing to his Lord until *'A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones'* was revealed.

I say: This is not correct for two reasons: First, this verse [the first one] clearly refers to the foremost (السابقين), and likewise the second refers to the People of the Right (أصحاب اليمين); do you not see how the People of the Right and their promise were coordinated with the foremost and their promise? Second, abrogation (النسخ) in reports (الأخبار) is not permissible." End quote.

It has been answered that the hadith can be interpreted to mean that when the companions heard the first verse, they thought that the matter in this Ummah would follow this course—that the People of the Right would be a large group from the former and a few from them—so the successful ones in Paradise from this Ummah would be fewer than those from previous nations. Then *'A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones'* was revealed, and their sadness vanished. The meaning of "abrogating" the previous verse would be the removal of their aforementioned thought.

However, you are well aware that this is an interpretation for which there is no evidence from the wording, and the wording rejects it—especially interpreting the abrogation of the verse as the removal of a thought. The state of the first narration, especially regarding its ending, is like the state of this narration.

In *al-Majma'*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: "They will be waited upon by immortal youths," there was a difference of opinion regarding these youths. It was said: they are the children of the people of the world who had no righteous deeds to be rewarded for, nor sins to be punished for, so they were placed in this station.

He said: It has been narrated from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) that he was asked about the children of the polytheists? He said: "They are the servants of the people of Paradise."

**I say:** It was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from al-Hasan, but the narration is weak and cannot be relied upon.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi al-Dunya narrated in *Sifat al-Jannah*, as did al-Bazzar, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *al-Ba'th* from 'Abdullah ibn Mas'ud who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said to me: 'Indeed, you will look at a bird in Paradise—and desire it—and it will fall before you roasted.'"

**I say:** There are many narrations with this meaning, and in some of them, the believer eats what he desires, then the remainder returns to what it was, comes to life, flies to its place, and boasts of that.

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "They will not hear therein any vain talk or sinful speech," he said: "Obscenity, lying, and singing (الغنا)."

**I say:** Perhaps what is meant by singing is that which is vain talk, or *al-ghina* (singing) is a scribal error for *al-khana* (obscenity).

And therein: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "And the People of the Right Hand—what are the People of the Right Hand?" he said: "'Ali ibn Abi Talib (عليه السلام) and his companions and his followers (شيعته)."

**I say:** The narration is based on what was mentioned regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَوْمَ نَدْعُوا كُلَّ أُناسٍ بِإِمامِهِمْ فَمَنْ أُوتِيَ كِتابَهُ بِيَمِينِهِ** — *“The day We shall summon every group of people with their imam, then whoever is given his book in his right hand...” (17:71)*; that the "Right" (اليمين) is the rightful Imam, and its meaning is that the "Right" is 'Ali (عليه السلام) and the "People of the Right" are his followers. The narration is of the type of application (الجري).

And therein: regarding His saying, the Exalted: "Amidst thornless lote trees," he said: "A tree that has no leaves and no thorns in it." And Abu 'Abdullah (عليه السلام) read: "and clustered fruit (وطلع منضود)" and said: "one upon another."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, al-Hakim narrated and authenticated it, as did al-Bayhaqi in *al-Ba'th* from Abu Umamah who said: "The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) used to say: 'Indeed, Allah benefits us through the Bedouins and their questions.' A Bedouin came one day and said: 'O Messenger of Allah—Allah has mentioned in the Qur'an a harmful tree. I did not think that in Paradise there would be a tree that harms its owner.' The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'And what is it?' He said: 'The lote tree, for it has thorns.' So the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: 'Does not Allah say: *“Amidst thornless lote trees”*? Allah removes its thorns—and places in the stead of every thorn a fruit—it grows fruit that opens up—into seventy-two colors of food—no color resembles the other.'"

In *al-Majma'*: It was narrated by the general public from 'Ali (عليه السلام): that a man read in his presence: "and clustered acacias (وَطَلْحٍ مَنْضُودٍ)," so he said: "What is the matter with the acacia? It is only 'clustered fruit' (وطلع) as in His saying: *'and palm trees with compact clusters'*." It was said to him: "Will you not change it?" He said: "The Qur'an is not to be disturbed today nor moved." It was narrated from him by his son al-Hasan (عليه السلام) and Qays ibn Sa'd.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, 'Abd al-Razzaq, al-Faryabi, Hannad, 'Abd ibn Humayd, Ibn Jarir, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from 'Ali ibn Abi Talib: regarding His saying: "and clustered acacias," he said: "It is the banana."

In *al-Majma'*: It was mentioned in a report: "Indeed, in Paradise there is a tree under whose shade a rider travels for a hundred years—without crossing it; read if you wish: *'and extended shade'*." It was also narrated: "The times of Paradise are like the mornings of summer—there is neither heat nor cold in them."

**I say:** The first was narrated in *al-Durr al-Manthur* from Abu Sa'id, Anas, and others from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم).

In *Rawdat al-Kafi*, with its chain from 'Ali ibn Ibrahim from Ibn Mahbub from Muhammad ibn Ishaq al-Madani from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): in a hadith describing Paradise and its people: "And they visit one another and enjoy themselves in their gardens—in an extended shade like that between the break of dawn—to the rising of the sun, and more pleasant than that."

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*: regarding His saying: "Indeed We have created them with a [special] creation," he said: "The wide-eyed houris in Paradise; *'and made them virgins, loving (عُرُباً)'*—he said: they do not speak except in Arabic."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Ja'far ibn Muhammad from his father who said: "The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: regarding His saying: *'loving (عُرُباً)'*—he said: 'Their speech is Arabic.'"

**I say:** There are other narrations stating that *'uruban* is the plural of *'arub*, which is the coquettish woman.

Therein: Musaddad narrated in his *Musnad*, as did Ibn al-Mundhir, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh with a good (حسن) chain from Abu Bakrah from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): regarding His saying, the Exalted: "A large group from the former peoples, and a large group from the later ones," he said: "They are both from this Ummah."

**I say:** This meaning is narrated in more than one narration, but the apparent meaning of the verses of the Surah is that the division is for all of humanity, not for this Ummah specifically. Perhaps what is meant by these narrations is the explanation of some instances (مصاديق), even if it is remote. Likewise is the meaning of what has been narrated that the People of the Right are the companions of the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), and what has been narrated that the People of the Left are the enemies of the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه السلام).

In *al-Mahasin*, with its chain from Mu'awiyah ibn Wahb from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه السلام) who said: "I asked him about drinking in one breath, and he disliked it—and said: 'That is the drinking of the *him* (الهيم).' I said: 'And what are the *him*?' He said: 'Camels.'"

And therein, with its chain from al-Halabi from Abu 'Abdullah (عليه السلام): that he used to dislike resembling the *him*. I said: "And what are the *him*?" He said: "The sand."

**I say:** Both meanings are mentioned in other narrations.
